title: ACLS – Asystole Cardiac Arrest
description: A patient presents with asystole, the non-shockable flat-line rhythm. Practise confirming asystole, applying the non-shockable ACLS algorithm, identifying and treating reversible causes (5H/5T), and understanding why defibrillation is contraindicated.
learningObjectives: Confirm asystole and avoid inappropriate defibrillation
Apply the ACLS non-shockable cardiac arrest algorithm
Administer epinephrine 1 mg IV every 3–5 minutes during asystole
Systematically search for and treat reversible causes (5H/5T)
Understand the poor prognosis of asystole and criteria for termination of resuscitation

states.prompt: A 70-year-old male with advanced COPD is found unresponsive in a nursing home. EMS confirms no pulse, no breathing. Monitor shows a nearly flat line with occasional artefact. How do you CONFIRM asystole before proceeding?

options.label: Check in two leads, verify gain settings, and confirm loose lead is not causing artefact
Defibrillate and see if rhythm converts
A single-lead flat line is sufficient confirmation
Wait 30 seconds for the rhythm to declare itself

explanations: Correct! AHA guidelines require asystole confirmation in ≥2 leads and verification that gain settings and lead connections are correct before concluding asystole.
Defibrillating asystole provides no benefit and may cause myocardial injury. Shock is only for shockable rhythms (VFib/VTach).
Single-lead flat line may represent fine VFib or a loose lead. Always confirm in multiple leads.
Waiting delays CPR. Confirm quickly in two leads while compressions continue.

checklistItems.description: Correctly confirms asystole in two leads before proceeding

states.prompt: Asystole confirmed in two leads. IV access obtained. What is the CORRECT immediate action for asystole?

options.label: Continue CPR and give epinephrine 1 mg IV as soon as access is available
Deliver a 200 J unsynchronised shock to restart the heart
Give atropine 1 mg IV as first-line drug for asystole

explanations: Correct! Non-shockable algorithm: CPR + epinephrine q3–5 min + treat reversible causes. No shock for asystole.
Defibrillation of asystole is contraindicated — the heart cannot be 'restarted' from a flat line by a shock.
Atropine is no longer recommended in cardiac arrest (removed from AHA guidelines 2010+). Epinephrine is the drug of choice.
Transcutaneous pacing has not been shown to improve outcomes in asystole and is not recommended as routine first-line treatment.

checklistItems.description: Correctly avoids defibrillation and gives epinephrine for asystole

states.prompt:
CPR is ongoing. Epinephrine 1 mg IV given at T+0. Good CPR quality: rate 105/min, depth 5.5 cm, full recoil, minimal interruptions.

📚 Teaching Point: Repeat epinephrine every 3–5 minutes throughout the arrest. Keep CPR cycles tight (2-min intervals with rhythm checks). Avoid hyperventilation – target 10 breaths/min with advanced airway.

Asystole continues despite 8 minutes of CPR and two doses of epinephrine. You systematically evaluate reversible causes. This patient has COPD and was found with an empty opioid prescription bottle nearby. Which 5H/5T cause should you treat NOW?

options.label: Hypoxia – ensure adequate oxygenation and ventilation
Tension pneumothorax – needle decompress both sides empirically
Toxins (opioid) – give naloxone 2 mg IV and reassess
Cardiac tamponade – perform bedside pericardiocentesis immediately

explanations: Correct! Hypoxia is the most common reversible cause in COPD patients with respiratory failure; ensure bag-mask ventilation is effective.
Tension pneumothorax is a consideration in COPD but requires clinical signs (absent breath sounds, tracheal deviation). Empirical bilateral decompression without these signs is not recommended.
Correct! With an empty opioid bottle present, opioid toxicity is a treatable cause. Naloxone 2 mg IV may restore spontaneous breathing.
Tamponade is possible but less likely without clinical context (neck veins, prior pericardial disease). Pericardiocentesis requires ultrasound confirmation when possible.

checklistItems.description: Correctly identifies and treats hypoxia and opioid toxicity

states.prompt: After naloxone and optimised ventilation, the rhythm converts to sinus bradycardia at 40 bpm. A weak carotid pulse is palpable. What is the correct IMMEDIATE post-ROSC management?

options.label: Optimise oxygenation (SpO2 94–98%), treat bradycardia if symptomatic, admit to ICU
Give atropine 1 mg for the bradycardia immediately
Resume CPR since HR < 60 is too slow for adequate output
Monitor for 1 hour in ED then discharge if stable

explanations: Correct! After ROSC, the priority is haemodynamic stabilisation, oxygenation, and ICU admission. Symptomatic bradycardia post-ROSC may be treated with atropine or pacing if causing haemodynamic compromise.
Atropine should only be given if the bradycardia is causing haemodynamic compromise. Routine atropine post-ROSC is not indicated.
ROSC with a palpable pulse is achieved – CPR must not be resumed unless the pulse is lost.
Post-cardiac arrest patients require ICU admission for post-resuscitation care (targeted temperature management, monitoring).

checklistItems.description: Correctly manages post-ROSC sinus bradycardia and plans ICU admission

states.prompt:
Scenario complete. Review your performance summary below.

📚 Key Teaching Points:
• Confirm asystole in ≥2 leads; NO defibrillation
• CPR + epinephrine 1 mg q3–5 min is the non-shockable algorithm
• Atropine is NOT recommended in cardiac arrest
• Always search for 5H/5T reversible causes throughout the arrest
